required.title: [Activity] A visit to a government building or a public meeting

suggested.title: [Look Together] Your city or town's website

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Your local government's organizational chart
[Activity] A family discussion about a recent local government decision

competence_markers.description: The child can name their mayor or equivalent local leader
They can identify at least three services that local government provides
They understand in basic terms why communities need shared rules and shared funding
They can explain the concept of taxes as community contribution
They demonstrate curiosity about how decisions are made in their community

narration_prompts.prompt: Who leads our city or town?
narration_prompts.detail: What is the mayor's name (or the town manager's name)? What do they do?

narration_prompts.prompt: Name three things that our local government provides.
narration_prompts.detail: How do these services affect your daily life?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do communities need rules for shared spaces?
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example of a rule that makes sense and explain why.

narration_prompts.prompt: Where does the government get the money to pay for things like roads and libraries?
narration_prompts.detail: Explain taxes in your own words.

narration_prompts.prompt: If you were the mayor, what is one thing you would do for our community?

cross_connections.description: The places the child mapped are products of the government decisions explored here.
1.4.1 asks why governments exist; this packet shows what governments actually do at the local level.
Understanding government services connects to understanding the rights and duties of citizens.
The concept of taxes connects to the financial literacy developed across 4.1.

cross_connections.target_name: Living as Part of a Community
cross_connections.description: Understanding government is part of the integrated civic awareness assessed in the capstone.

content.why_this_matters:
Look around your town or neighborhood. The road you drive on — someone decided to build it there. The park you play in — someone decided to create it. The fire truck that comes when you call 911 — someone decided to buy it and hire the firefighters who drive it. The school you attend, the library you visit, the streetlights that turn on at night — all of these exist because real people made real decisions.

Those people are your local government. They are not distant strangers in a faraway capital. They are people who live in your community, who go to the same grocery store you do, and who make decisions every week that affect your daily life. Most people — even most grown-ups — have no idea who these people are or what they do. You are going to learn.

Understanding government is not about memorizing facts for a test. It is about understanding who is in charge of the place you live and how the things around you came to be. When you know who decides, you know who to talk to when something needs to change.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness that government is real and local** — not an abstract concept but real people making real decisions about real places
2. **Identification of government services** — recognizing the public services that shape daily life
3. **Understanding of shared rules** — why communities need agreed-upon rules for shared spaces
4. **Basic concept of taxes as contribution** — understanding that shared services require shared funding
5. **Connection to real officials** — knowing the name of at least one local leader
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Government Is People Making Decisions

When you hear the word "government," you might think of the President or Congress — the people you see on the news in Washington, D.C. But the government that affects your daily life the most is not in Washington. It is right here, in your town or city.

Your local government is made up of real people who live in your community. They make decisions about things that matter to you every day:

- **Should the city build a new park, or fix the old one?** That is a local government decision.
- **How fast can cars drive on your street?** That is a local government decision.
- **Should the library stay open on Saturdays?** That is a local government decision.
- **Where should the new school be built?** That is a local government decision.
- **How many firefighters does the city need?** That is a local government decision.

The most important local leader is usually the **mayor** — the person elected to lead the city or town. In some places, instead of a mayor, there is a **town manager** or a **city manager** who is hired to do the job. Either way, this person is responsible for making sure the community works.

The mayor does not work alone. There is usually a **city council** or **town council** — a group of people elected by the community to make decisions together. They meet regularly — usually every week or two — and they discuss, argue about, and vote on the issues that affect the community.

**Your task:** Find out who leads your community. What is your mayor's name? If you have a city council, who is on it? This information is on your city or town's website, and it is worth knowing.

### Part 2: What Government Does for You Every Day

Government provides services — things that everyone in the community uses and that would be very difficult for any one person or family to provide on their own. You use government services every day, even if you do not notice them.

**Roads and sidewalks.** The street in front of your house was built and is maintained by the government. When it snows, government trucks plow the roads. When a pothole appears, the government fills it (eventually). When a new neighborhood is built, the government approves the roads and makes sure they connect to everything else.

**Water and sewage.** When you turn on the faucet, clean water comes out. When you flush the toilet, the waste goes somewhere and is treated. Both of these things happen because the government built and maintains the water and sewer systems. This is one of the most important things government does, and one of the least noticed.

**Fire and emergency services.** When you call 911, someone answers. Firefighters, paramedics, or police officers come to help. These people are government employees, paid with community money, on call around the clock.

**Parks and recreation.** The parks where you play, the trails where you hike, the community pool where you swim in summer — these are built and maintained by the government for everyone to use.

**Libraries.** Your public library is a government service — free, open to everyone, filled with books, computers, programs, and people who can help you find information. It is one of the best things government does.

**Schools.** If you attend public school, the building, the teachers, the books, and the bus that gets you there are all provided through government — specifically, through the school district, which is governed by a school board.

### Part 3: Rules for Shared Spaces

Your family has rules. Maybe you have to take off your shoes inside, or clean up after dinner, or be quiet after bedtime. These rules exist because you share a space with other people, and the rules help everyone get along.

Communities have rules for the same reason, but on a bigger scale. When thousands of people share roads, parks, schools, and neighborhoods, they need agreed-upon rules so that everyone can use those spaces safely and fairly.

**Some rules make sense right away:**
- Cars stop at red lights so people do not crash into each other.
- You cannot play loud music at 3 AM because your neighbors are sleeping.
- Dogs must be on leashes in the park because not everyone is comfortable around dogs.
- You cannot dump trash in the river because everyone downstream drinks that water.

**Some rules require more thought:**
- How tall can a building be? (Too tall, and it blocks the sun for everyone behind it. Too short, and you cannot fit enough homes for people who need them.)
- Can someone open a factory next to a school? (Probably not — but who decides where factories can go?)
- How fast should cars drive on a residential street? (Fast is convenient. Slow is safe. The rule is a balance.)

These are the kinds of decisions your local government makes. Not everyone agrees on the answers. That is why government meetings involve discussion, debate, and voting — people with different opinions work through their disagreements to reach a decision that the community can live with.

**Discussion question:** Can you think of a rule in your community that you think is good? Can you think of one that you think could be better? Why?

### Part 4: Taxes — How the Community Pays for Shared Things

All of those services — roads, firefighters, libraries, parks, water — cost money. Where does the money come from? From the people who live in the community. This money is called **taxes**.

Taxes are not a punishment. They are how a community pays for the things it shares. Think of it this way: if your family wanted to build a swimming pool just for your family, it would cost a lot of money. But if every family in the neighborhood put in a little money, they could build a public pool that everyone can use. That is essentially how taxes work — everyone contributes a little so the community can have things that no one family could afford alone.

There are different kinds of taxes:
- **Property taxes** are paid by people who own homes or land. This is the main way most local governments raise money.
- **Sales taxes** are added to things you buy at the store. A small portion of every purchase goes to the government.
- **Income taxes** are based on how much money a person earns. This is mostly a state and federal government tax, but some cities have local income taxes too.

The local government decides how to spend the tax money. How much goes to police and fire? How much goes to schools? How much goes to parks? How much goes to road repair? These spending decisions are made in the **budget** — a document that says exactly how much money the government expects to receive and how it plans to spend it.

**The connection to you:** Every time your family buys something at the store, a small amount of that purchase helps pay for the fire truck, the library books, and the park swings. You are already contributing to your community, just by living in it.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Local Leader Identification
**Developing well:** The child can name their mayor (or equivalent) and has some understanding of what that person does. Ideally, the child has seen this person in some context — a photo, a meeting, a visit.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot name any local official. Government remains abstract.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you looked up the mayor's name together? Have you visited the city website? A simple five-minute search makes this concrete.

### 2. Government Service Recognition
**Developing well:** The child can name at least three government services and explain how they affect daily life. They connect visible features of their environment (roads, parks, libraries) to government action.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot name government services or does not connect the things around them to government decisions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you pointed out government services during daily life? "See that road? The city built it. See that fire station? The city pays for it." Repetition in context builds recognition.

### 3. Shared Rules Understanding
**Developing well:** The child understands that communities need rules for shared spaces and can give examples. They can articulate that not everyone agrees on rules and that discussion is needed.

**Developing slowly:** Rules feel like impositions rather than agreements. No connection between family rules and community rules.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you connected household rules to community rules explicitly? "At home, we take turns with the TV. In the community, we take turns at the four-way stop." The analogy makes the concept click.
